data.title: Bad Breadth Concerns Rise As S&P 500 Set To Recover Tariff-Triggered Losses
data.description:
After stumbling to its lowest point in nearly a year this April, the S&P 500 has recovered nearly all its losses in roughly two months. However, the outsized returns of tech stocks have driven fresh concerns over a lack of market breadth that could undermine the rally's lasting power.

"I would not describe this rally as healthy," Michael O'Rourke, chief market strategist at JonesTrading, said in an interview.

After reaching an all-time high on Feb. 19, the S&P 500 tumbled 18.9%, reaching a bottom on April 8 after US President Donald Trump's announcement of tariffs on nearly all trading partners rattled investors. The large-cap index has since risen more than 20.5% through June 9, and as of June 10, was less than 2% from matching that all-time high again.

However, the rally is less impressive when excluding the mega-cap technology stocks, known as the Magnificent Seven, which have weighed heavily on the S&P 500's performance over roughly the past five years. As of June 9, these stocks — NVIDIA Corp., Tesla Inc., Meta Platforms Inc., Microsoft Corp., Amazon.com Inc., Alphabet Inc. and Apple Inc. — were up by an average of 31.7% from April 8, after declining by an average of 26.8% from Feb. 19 through April 8.

While the S&P 500 has rallied 20.5% since April 8, that gain is 16.9% if those seven mega-cap stocks are removed.

"They are the largest and most liquid names, as well as respected companies, so they attracted significant flows over the short period of time and fueled index performance," said O'Rourke.

The ongoing broader market rally has been driven by investors and speculators chasing market exposure amid expectations that the worst of the Trump trade policy headwinds may be over and has pushed US equities back to historically expensive levels, according to O'Rourke.

The S&P 500's IT and communication services sectors fared the best during this rally, gaining 33.7% and 25%, respectively, since April 8.

Without these two tech sectors, the S&P 500 ended June 9 up 14.5% from April 8.

Market breadth has deteriorated, with 49% of S&P 500 stocks trading above their 200-day moving averages, compared to 59% trading above those averages in February, according to Tom Essaye, founder of Sevens Report Research.

"If the rally were sustainable, we would hope to see better participation," Essaye wrote in a June 9 note.

The concentration may not ultimately doom this rally, however, said George Pearkes, a macro strategist at Bespoke Investment Group.

"On the one hand, those are the stocks that are generating earnings growth; it would be more concerning if they were showing some sort of weakness in their actual results while valuations expanded," Pearkes said in an interview. "But that isn't really what we're seeing. It's unprecedented to see this kind of top-heavy earnings growth, but markets reflecting that isn't a sign of the price action being unhealthy."

Ultimately, lack of breadth may not matter as much as complacency in trading, said O'Rourke with JonesTrading.

"Markets have rallied on trade optimism, but employment is exhibiting signs of slowing," O'Rourke said. "If an economic slowdown materializes as I expect this summer, then investors who chased this rally are likely to be disappointed."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: AGCO Corporation: Valuation Is High, And There Are Too Many Uncertainties
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on AGCO Corporation (NYSE:AGCO), which I downgraded to a hold rating as I lost confidence in the near-term turnaround potential, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. While there are promising signs like the recovery in South America and cost discipline, my view remains cautious overall. The situation in North America is still tough, inventory levels are elevated, and the tariff overhang only adds to the uncertainty. These factors, alongside a high valuation, make it tough to invest in AGCO today.

Investment thesis

The near-term outlook for AGCO remains mixed. The demand environment in North America remains soft, and management guidance—calling for a steep 25% to 30% y/y decline—is a good indication of the current situation. The same issue I flagged is still persistent (dealer inventory problem), which, despite aggressive production cuts—AGCO literally slashed North American output by >50% in 1Q25 and has similar cuts planned for 2Q25—channel inventory remains high. To give a sense of how high, consider that despite the cuts and making good progress in destocking large ag units by ~7%, the blended level is still ~8.5 months of supply versus a six-month target—which is similar in February (I noted three months above target).

The good news here for North America is I do see a potential leading indicator for a turnaround. Recent survey data from early June shows that US farmer sentiment has surged to a four-year-high. This is a dramatic shift from the poor sentiment in the past few months, which seems to be based on a more optimistic outlook on agricultural exports and a less negative view on the impact of tariffs. The problem I have is this improved sentiment has clearly not translated into a material recovery in equipment orders yet. And I think a critical assumption here that is uncertain is the impact of tariffs. As of today, nobody really knows the outcome of the trade negotiations. If it turns out well, this optimism should quickly turn into orders and show up in AGCO financials. But the same goes for the flip side: if we go into a full-blown trade war, expectations will turn very quickly.

As for Europe, AGCO's largest market, it is also facing massive headwinds. My expectation here is that demand remains weak because the underlying drivers for the sharp 23% y/y constant currency sales decline seen in 1Q25 are still in place. Key Western European markets like France and Germany continue to struggle, and farmer profitability continues to face pressure from high input costs and uncertain growing conditions, which will likely continue to hurt demand for new equipment.

Tariff Risks

More on the tariff risk, I believe this risk is substantial and really makes the near-term outlook more uncertain. Management estimates that tariffs will have a ~$0.30 per share negative impact this year, and the bulk of this stems from the 10% US tariff on EU imports. This is significant given that ~25% of AGCO's $2.8 billion North American sales in 2024 were tied to European-made equipment. Note management explicitly called out the tariff on EU imports as the main cause for this $0.30 headwind. My concern here is the ambiguity of the impact on the cost of Chinese-sourced components. We have seen how high the tariff rates can go up to (between the US and China), and I am not saying it will get there again, but if it just lands in the middle between the current rate and that high rate (>100%), AGCO's cost of goods will likely go through the roof, and this will crush margins. While AGCO is working to offset these effects through supply chain adjustments, they did note that their pricing response could trigger an incremental slowdown to the North American business. And this really shows how weak the underlying demand is.

Operational Discipline and Brazil's Potential Turnaround

That said, there are some very encouraging developments that deserve highlighting and make the overall equity story less negative. The most encouraging development is the strength in South America, which gives hope that there is still light at the end of the tunnel. In Brazil, retail tractor sales grew 11% y/y in 1Q25, and the momentum has clearly followed through, as management had the confidence to revise the FY25 regional outlook from flat to as much as 5% growth. This was against the backdrop of stronger farmer sentiment, and the idea is that North America could be in a similar phase before growth inflecting upwards.

The other area that, I thought, was very encouraging, and especially good for the longer term, is management's ability to optimize AGCO's cost structure. In 1Q25, AGCO managed to aggressively cut production hours by 33% y/y in 1Q25, and despite that, it only saw low-to-mid 20% decremental margins. You would imagine decremental margins to be way higher given the fixed cost base. This tells us that whatever cost-saving initiatives employed have worked (the expectation is for these actions to yield $100-125 million in run-rate savings). This sets up a very positive mid-term outlook when demand recovers, as it means more room for margin to expand as AGCO can now operate at a lower cost structure.

Valuation

My view on AGCO's valuation remains largely the same, in that the market is already pricing in a recovery. I am still of the view that the near-term outlook is too uncertain, and I would think it has gotten worse vs. in Feb'25 because of the tariff situation. For AGCO multiples to go down from here, all we need is for the US and China trade talks to turn sour, and while nobody knows if this will happen, this is an actual tangible risk that should not be ignored (the market is apparently overlooking this, based on the valuation). Even if I were to play devil's advocate and say that the bull case happens (demand comes back, and tariff issues are gone), the upside to multiple is also not very exciting. The highest multiple that AGCO has ever traded at was 25x in FY17, or ~25% upside from here. The downside could be as low as 10x (AGCO was here 12 months ago).

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for AGCO remains a hold. While there are valid reasons to be positive about a potential medium-term recovery, the near term is too uncertain. The tariff overhang, lack of clear demand inflection, and continued inventory excess make the near-term outlook hard to predict. Moreover, valuation already reflects some optimism, which I think limits the risk-reward.

data.title: How Much Does Income Matter For Total Return? It Depends On The Asset
data.description:
When comparing asset class performance, one common point of confusion for new investors comes from the benchmark used. There are typically two versions of an index: the price return and the total return.

The price return measures only the change in the index’s market value. The total return version, by contrast, includes both price changes and any income distributions, such as dividends or interest, assuming they’re reinvested.

In virtually every case, the total return will outperform the price return over time because it reflects the compounding benefit of reinvested income. But how large that difference is can vary significantly.

For some assets, income plays a modest role. For others, it’s the main driver of total return. This makes it a useful lens for understanding how the reinvestment of that income matters. It becomes especially important in real-world applications where tax drag becomes a variable.

Here’s a comparison across three major income-generating asset classes: U.S. large-cap equities, master limited partnerships (MLPs), and real estate investment trusts (REITs).

U.S. Large-Cap Stocks

Over the past decade, the S&P 500 has delivered impressive gains, but much of that return has come from price appreciation.

From early 2015 through mid-2025, the index’s cumulative price return was 183.1%, while its total return was 238.4%. That means reinvested dividends accounted for roughly 23% of the total return.

This reinforces the idea that while income matters, its role in U.S. large-cap equities has historically been secondary to price growth.

That’s partly by design: many S&P 500 companies now prioritize capital reinvestment, share buybacks, and acquisitions over high dividend payouts.

Management teams have also become increasingly shareholder-friendly through stock repurchase programs, which boost earnings per share and support higher valuations.

U.S. Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs)

The CRSP US REIT Index paints a striking picture of how meaningful income can be for total returns. From 2015 to 2025, the index’s price return was just 29.94%, while the total return was 85.98%. Roughly two-thirds of the total return came from reinvested distributions.

This outsized income effect is no coincidence. Real estate investment trusts (REITs) are legally required to distribute at least 90% of their taxable income to shareholders in the form of dividends.

This rule allows them to avoid corporate income tax, but it also means REITs tend to offer much higher yields than traditional equities.

Unlike the S&P 500, where capital appreciation tends to carry the load, REITs rely on a stream of predictable, recurring cash flows generated by long-term leases.

While their price can swing due to interest rates or sector sentiment, the dividend engine under the hood keeps turning, and for investors who stay invested, that income becomes a compounding asset all its own.

Master Limited Partnerships (MLPs)

The following chart may be the most dramatic illustration of all. From 2015 to 2025, the Alerian MLP Index price return was -29.6%, while the total return version of the same index delivered a positive +58.4%. It all comes down to income.

MLPs are among the highest-yielding assets in public markets, regularly offering yields north of 6% to 8%. Unlike equities, which may retain earnings for buybacks or reinvestment, MLPs are explicitly designed to pass through the bulk of their distributable cash flow.

That’s why price alone paints a misleading picture. In this case, price returns were weighed down by years of energy market volatility, regulatory risks, and investor exits following the 2014 oil crash. But for long-term holders reinvesting distributions, total returns remained solidly positive.

The underlying cash flow from fee-based pipeline infrastructure persisted and investors who understood the income component were rewarded, even when headline prices lagged.

It’s a striking reminder: income-heavy asset classes can defy price action. Ignore distributions, and you miss the entire point of owning MLPs.

Disclosure

This information is not an offer to sell, or solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product, security, or services offered by Jay Hatfield, or Infrastructure Capital Advisors, LLC, (“ICA”) or its affiliates. ICA, will only conduct such solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product or service offered by ICA, if at all, by (1) purported definitive documentation (which will include disclosures relating to investment objective, policies, risk factors, fees, tax implications and relevant qualifications), (2) to qualified participants, if applicable, and (3) only in those jurisdictions where permitted by law. Jay Hatfield or ICA may have a beneficial long or short position in securities discussed either through stock ownership, options, or other derivatives; nonetheless, under no circumstances does any article or interview represent a recommendation to buy or sell these securities. This discussion is intended to provide insight into stocks and the market for entertainment and information purposes only and is not a solicitation of any kind. ICA buys and sells securities on behalf of its fund investors and may do so, before and after any particular article herein is published, with respect to the securities discussed in any article posted. ICA's appraisal of a company (price target) is only one factor that affects its decision whether to buy or sell shares in that company. Other factors might include, but are not limited to, the presence of mandatory limits on individual positions, decisions regarding portfolio exposures, and general market conditions and liquidity needs. As such, there may not always be consistency between the views expressed here and ICA's trading or holdings on behalf of its fund investors. There may be conflicts between the content posted or discussed and the interests of ICA. Please reach out to the ICA for more information. Investors should make their own decisions regarding any investments mentioned, and their prospects based on such investors’ own review of publicly available information and should not rely on the information contained herein. ICA nor any of its affiliates accepts any liability whatsoever for any direct or consequential loss howsoever arising, directly or indirectly, from any use of the information contained herein. We have not sought, nor have we received, permission from any third-party to include their information in this article. Certain information contained in this document constitutes “forward-looking statements,” which can be identified by the use of forward-looking terminology such as “may,” “will,” “should,” “expect,” “anticipate,” “project,” “estimate,” “intend,” “continue” or “believe” or the negatives thereof or other variations thereon or other comparable terminology. Due to various risks and uncertainties, actual events or results may differ materially from those reflected or contemplated in such forward-looking statements. The information contained herein represents our subjective belief and opinions and should not be construed as investment, tax, legal, or financial advice.

Original Source: Infrastructure Capital Advisors

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Stitch Fix: Continuing To Lose Customers In Increasingly Competitive Market
data.description:
I have fond memories of my use of the now-defunct online grocer Webvan. I remember sitting in my home at night and selecting groceries that would be brought to my door the following day. The company was always on time with its deliveries. The produce seemed to be of high quality. The steaks often appeared nicer than anything I could find on my own at my local grocery store. Sadly, the company’s expenses could never keep up with its revenues. In the end, high costs, logistical limitations, and its inability to offer a premium price for a service that most people were perfectly capable of doing on their own was its downfall.

While the two companies are not identical, I cannot help but reflect on my experience with Webvan as I think about Stitch Fix (NASDAQ: SFIX). In theory, the company seems to be providing a service that would be in high demand as people pay for the convenience of a personal shopper for clothing and accessories. However, the company is caught between growing its customer base and offering prices that truly capture all of its expenses. If SFIX raises prices, it will lose customers. If prices stay the same, the company runs the risk of being the next tombstone in the online retail graveyard. As seen below, revenues have been on a steady decline since the pandemic-related online shopping boom in 2021.

The most recent quarterly results give investors a glimmer of hope, but do little to alleviate my biggest concerns about the company’s business. I would avoid buying SFIX until the company shows it can make meaningful headway in approaching profitability in what has become an incredibly competitive retail market.

Company Snapshot

Stitch Fix is a clothing retailer that offers online personal shopping and home delivery for men, women, and children. Customers begin their relationship with Stitch Fix by taking a quiz about their sizes, style preferences, and target price range. For a $20 styling fee, SFIX then ships items to the customer to try on in their own home. Unwanted clothes are returned to Stitch Fix with free shipping. Clothes that are kept must be paid for by the customer, with the styling fee applied as a credit. The company also offers direct purchase of individual items from its website.

SFIX currently has a market cap of $610 million. While the stock trades at a price hovering around $5 per share today, it once peaked at just over $100 per share in January 2021 when lock-downs and social distancing made everyone think that real, in-person shopping could become extinct. The company reported 2.5 million active clients in its last fiscal year, but that number includes any customer who received as few as one item from Stitch Fix in the last 12 months.

Total revenue for the company is FY 2024 was $1.34 billion. The company is projecting FY 2025 to bring in between $1.245 and $1.249 billion, which would make this year the fourth straight for declining top line numbers. Gross margins have been improving for the company. The margin for FY 2024 was the highest for the company since FY 2021. Adjusted EBITDA margin also improved as the company is making a concerted effort to reduce costs. However, the decline in active clients and the fall off in revenues are happening faster than SFIX’s other improvements.

Analysis of SFIX

On June 10, Stitch Fix released its Q3 2025 results. (The company has a unique fiscal year that ends on the Saturday closest to July 31.) The results were not good, but they were not as bad as expected. A current investor in SFIX can still see a light at the end of the tunnel, but it is a very faint light. The market responded positively, at least initially, to the numbers as the stock was up 7.5% after hours once the earnings call took place.

The company lost $0.06 per share, but that was $0.05 better than expected and $0.12 better than the same quarter last year. Stitch Fix reported $325 million in revenue, which was roughly $11.5 million better than the company’s own projections from just three months ago. Revenue per client was $542, an increase of 0.9% from the last quarter and 3.2% from Q3 2024. (The annual gain is only 0.8% based on the CPI numbers that were released on June 11.) Gross margins were down 0.3% from the last quarter and 1.3% from the same period last year at 44.2%. The company blamed lower product margins on the decline.

The graphs below sum up the current situation for the company pretty well. The company is losing customers on a consistent basis. Active clients in the most recent quarter totaled 2.35 million, and that number has fallen as far back as the company’s earnings presentation will go. (The decline goes back to Q1 2022.) The rate of decline is slowing, but it is still a decline.

Gross margins have been up and down, but revenue finally increased this quarter after five straight quarters of declines. One of the biggest problems now is that the cost of acquiring these customers is increasing steadily. Advertising as a percent of revenue was 10.2% in Q3 2025, the first time that number has been in the double digits in two years.

One other point of concern for the company is its steadily increasing share count. While the company may be debt-free, it is paying for costs with added shares, diluting the value of ownership for current shareholders. The book value for the stock has been declining as well, although that slide may be leveling out this year.

YEAR

SHARE COUNT

% INCREASE

BOOK VALUE/ SHARE

At the end of Q2 2025, the company ended the quarter with no debt and $230 million in cash. Cash and cash equivalents were up in Q3 at $242 million. The lack of debt is an important piece of the SFIX story, and the company expects to be cash flow positive for the full year.

Company CEO Matt Baer said the following about the most recent quarter:

Our performance, which exceeded expectations, is the direct result of the strength of the Stitch Fix value proposition and the team's disciplined execution of our strategy. Now in the growth phase of our transformation, we are focused on cementing our role as the retailer of choice for apparel and accessories by consistently delivering the most client-centric and personalized shopping experience.

A VERY Crowded Market

There was a time, early in the life of Stitch Fix, that it was the only player in this space. Like Webvan in the late 1990s, SFIX was able to grow its customer base quickly and was not under threat of meaningful competition. Today, there are a number of companies that do basically the same thing, utilizing various forms of technology, including AI, to deliver a personalized shopping experience for its customers.

Wantable offers services very similar to Stitch Fix for both men and women. The company offers both name brand options as well as in-house brands. Menlo Club is a similar service available only for men. This company offers both monthly and seasonal subscription options. Then there is Dia & Co. for plus-sized women, Curateur for high-end women’s apparel, and several other companies that offer clothing rentals instead of purchases.

It is interesting to note that the biggest name in the game, Amazon (AMZN), shut down its Prime Wardrobe service earlier this year. On the surface, this could be good news for Stitch Fix, but it was the reasoning behind Amazon's decision that should give SFIX shareholders pause. Amazon discovered that its customers were able to use its AI features to basically create their own virtual shopper. One such feature, the Virtual Try-On, allows customers to put 3-D images of shoes on their own feet using a smartphone camera. In other words, Amazon’s own service could not add value to the tools that already excited on its site.

It is clear that Stitch Fix is now in a very competitive market at a time when almost everyone has access to some basic form of AI that can come close to replicating their services. Because SFIX does not offer any unique item or service that others cannot offer, its moat has become quite narrow. Price is likely the most significant lever for competition. Given the struggles the company has with profits, this does not paint a great picture for SFIX's future. In its recent earnings presentations, the company likes to tout the potential for expansion for the entire apparel market, but what is not shown in images like this are all of the other players in this space.

Risks to Consider

There is a risk that Stitch Fix will never be able to reach profitability. The company has consistently reported earnings losses at the end of its fiscal year, even during the boom of the pandemic. Shrinking demand will make it challenging for the company to have a meaningful turnaround in its business any time soon. This stock is only appropriate for investors who can tolerate a high level of risk and volatility.

As mentioned earlier, Stitch Fix is competing in a retail space that is highly competitive. In order to attract new business and keep existing customers, the company may need to give away discounts or incentives. Margins that are already under pressure may be more strained if one or more competitors in the market decide to lower their prices.

Stitch Fix is also subject to possible declines in business activity if the overall US economy has a significant slowdown in the second half of 2025. The company used to have operations in the United Kingdom, but that division was shut down during its last fiscal year because of poor sales and an inability to control costs. That leaves the company fully dependent on the North American consumer to pull it out of its current hole.

Conclusion

Like Webvan, Stitch Fix may have simply pushed a great idea to the market too soon. Online grocery delivery is certainly here to stay, and the lessons learned from Webvan’s collapse may be one of the biggest boosts to the success of current players in that space. Stitch Fix started with a unique idea, and the timing of the pandemic may have led people to believe that its business model could really be a long-term success. The company is debt free, so unlike Webvan, it is not burdened with additional costs and may survive on life support for some time. There are just so many other players in the market now, and they have the benefit from learning from Stitch Fix’s mistakes. The democratization of AI is also an issue that has decreased the value of the services that SFIX offers.

The only reason I am rating the stock has a hold is because the company does seem to be making slight improvements in a few key areas. However, if the company is going to survive, its turnaround will not happen overnight. This stock is only a hold for those investors who can handle the fact that they are basically buying an online retail lottery ticket with a cloudy outlook of getting all of the numbers right.

data.title: IETC: Fantastic Companies, But Not Great Prices - Hold
data.description:
Introduction

iShares U.S. Tech Independence Focused ETF (IETC) is a fund that aims to gain exposure to US technology stocks - those with higher proportion of revenues, capabilities, and production based in the US. They aim to own companies that leverage big data, AI, and other digital economy plays. Below are some key facts of the fund, taken from the prospectus.

  • Expense Ratio: 0.18%
  • AUM: $609.5M
  • Average Volume: 94K

Their top holdings are shown below.

Their breakdown of holdings by sector is shown below.

We are very bullish on the idea of this fund, like that it is cheaper (0.18% for a thematic ETF is relatively lower) and the fund is invested in US tech names - a sector that has some great names.

Top Holdings Analysis

We highlight the top 3 holdings - which also happen to be names that we are very bullish on.

Broadcom (AVGO): AVGO is a leading networking and custom AI chip play that has already started to encroach on the territory of the main GPU leaders, as they provide a custom solution that many hyper scalers view as more cost-effective per use case. Their latest earnings reveal that revenue increased 46% from the year prior, which is a large jump up and shows that they have a strong base of products that hyper scalers can use to build up their AI investments further. The company has a strong pipeline of potential customers ahead, as more companies look to diversify from a purely GPU-based AI strategy. Google is one of its customers, and they are using AVGO products for networking for their data centers - and rate it so highly that AVGO was awarded Google’s Cloud Partner of the Year for the 5th year in a row. This partnership, honed over a few years now, shows how AVGO’s products are still leading the networking and custom chip market, and should be a good portion of a portfolio in addition to other AI chip plays. With companies such as Meta, Apple, ByteDance, and many other leading names using AVGO chips in their portfolio, AVGO stands out as a great name to own. The chart below shows the growth of AVGO’s AI revenue over the past 3 years, and it certainly shows no sign of slowing down.

We are firmly bullish on AVGO and like to see it included in this ETF as a top holding.

Nvidia (NVDA): Nvidia is winning both the consumer gaming and AI space. For gaming, they are powering the new Switch 2 from Nintendo and this is a hyped console that should get plenty of orders and customers. In fact, many sources are saying that the Switch 2 sold 3 million units on launch day alone, which is record-breaking and just shows the widespread reach that being associated with this product would have for NVDA. The company does not even need it though - as the latest survey of PC gamers revealed that NVDA’s GPUs are at an all-time high in market share - they now own 92% of the consumer gaming market!

The graph here shows the large discrepancy between NVDA and its GPU peers - the gap is simply insurmountable at this level. NVDA is truly crushing it in the consumer side of things, and that is also the case with their enterprise AI solutions. The firm, along with Dell, announced a massive partnership in building a new supercomputer for the US Department of Energy, named Doudna, which will go live in 2026. This computer will leverage the next-gen Rubin class of chips and will provide further share for NVDA in the space. It will help with cutting-edge sectors, such as molecular dynamics and physics research, and these are not only fields that have widespread implications for science, but more so for the entire human race. Being involved in this space is absolutely a positive for all of the companies involved, including NVDA. They are a leader in the consumer and wholesale markets and show no signs of letting up their lead. Inclusion in this ETF shows the market still values NVDA as the leader in this industry.

Palantir (PLTR): PLTR remains a leader in actually using AI in the real world. They are consistently signing new deals and showing off their tech to clients both in the US and abroad. Just today, they expanded their logistics AI footprint with a deal with Fedrigoni, a large Italian firm known for premium logistics operations in over 28 countries. They are at the forefront of delivering utility to customers all around the world, and they are gaining more and more data to further influence their AI models and decision-making. The main part of PLTR that excites us, however, is not their private sector work, but more so, their public sector defense related projects. They are in the bidding for being a core vendor in the Golden Dome project, one that could be incredibly lucrative for the firm as most government contracts usually are. As defense spending continues to rise, there is more and more demand, globally, for PLTR’s AI-backed defensive capabilities. It was revealed that PLTR, SpaceX, and Anduril are collaborating on a satellite constellation missile protection system that would cover the US, and these ~1,000 satellites would be specialized in protection from the latest in missile tech - hypersonic missiles. Being involved with such a project is of course very bullish for PLTR, which should help the high-flying stock grow even more. With the backdrop of defense spending growing every year, and the threats around the world that the US faces, this bid currently may very well turn in to a real project soon.

PLTR being involved in the Golden Dome is a great sign that their work is being realized as something that can add massive value to corporations and governments alike. They are poised for success under an administration that is determined to develop new military technologies and are being pushed by macro factors (global instability). PLTR being included in this ETF is a great sign and something that we support.

Valuation

In order to understand IETC's current valuation and if it is seemingly undervalued or overvalued, we look at the top holdings and understand their valuation. For this analysis, we use the P/E ratio of the top holdings and compare it to their historical levels. We find that a ratio like P/E is a great indicator of relative valuation as its meaning does not change over time. A P/E ratio of 15 in the year 2000 means the same as a P/E ratio of 15 in the year 2020. Inflation and other similar factors do not impact this. For this case, we look at the P/E ratio of the top 3 holdings of IETC over the last 3 years:

For PLTR, we only have a P/E ratio going back to October 2nd, 2023 as they had negative net income before then.

We look at the P/E growth rates of AVGO and NVDA across the full 3-year period, but in order to create a more direct comparison, we also look at the growth since October 2023.

Ticker

P/E Growth since October 2023

N/A

149.37%

Based on the above, we believe that all 3 companies are either overvalued (AVGO, PLTR) or near fair value (NVDA). Let's break down each one:

  • AVGO - AVGO’s P/E has increased by over 200% in both timeframes we looked at. This is a huge increase in such a short period of time.

  • NVDA - While NVDA shows a big decline in P/E since October 2023, this is mostly attributable to a large increase in 2023 that culminated in July of 2023. In this scenario, we find that the 3-year growth is more indicative of NVDA's relative valuation as it is not impacted by this huge increase. With only a 2% increase over the 3 years, we see NVDA as near fair value based on this analysis.

  • PLTR - With a 149% growth in less than 3 years, it's clear that PLTR is overvalued compared to its history.

Even with NVDA looking to be near fair value, the lofty valuations on both AVGO give us pause as we consider IETC.

In order to solidify our thesis, we look at the next four largest names in IETC. These 4 along with the top 3 names together make up ~50% of the ETF.

Ticker

3-Year P/E Growth

P/E Growth since October 2023

AMZN

-32.68%

-45.44%

MSFT

37.79%

16.71%

CRM

-76.19%

-67.12%

ORCL

49.57%

31.53%

With this additional data, it is a mixed bag. We have some names like MSFT and ORCL that have shown increases in P/E and others such as AMZN and CRM that have shown declines. In the case of CRM, we believe that similar to NVDA, the big decline in P/E could be misleading due to a large anomalous increase in 2023. If we instead look at a 5-year range, we see that CRMs P/E has declined by only 44%. While we still consider it undervalued per our metric, it is not ~70% undervalued as the initial metrics might suggest.

Overall, based on the information we see, we consider IETC to be overvalued. Yes, there are names such as AMZN and CRM that have dropped in P/E and there is also NVDA that has shown a flat P/E. However, most of the companies we looked at had a growth in P/E and especially 2 of the top 3 names (AVGO and PLTR) had very huge growth.

Risks

A major risk to the thesis is that many of these top names are AI focused, tech names - these are usually high-flying names that run up quickly and may sometimes experience extreme volatility. The betas of the top 3 names certainly reflect that: AVGO is 1.12, NVDA is 2.12, and PLTR is even higher at 2.64. This is something to consider as a major risk because none of these names would be considered “safe haven” investments in the chance that something negative happens, such as a recession. In fact, these names would drop more than the market average due to their high valuations as tech names and higher relative betas compared to the market. A rotation out of AI investments would be significantly damaging to the fund’s underlying names, and could pose a major risk to our thesis of being bullish on the companies in the fund.

Conclusion

We are generally bullish on the companies in IETC due to strong AI leadership in the top holdings. Despite the risks we discussed related to a potential recession or a rotation out of AI investments, we believe that the AI space will remain strong and demand across all levels of the supply chain will only continue to grow.

However, based on our valuation analysis, we see that most of IETC's top holdings are overvalued compared to their historical valuations with some trading at significantly higher valuations compared to their historical levels. Considering that we are strongly bullish on the underlying businesses and growth in the AI sector, we rate IETC as a hold. We believe that the future potential growth in AI will help to justify the high valuations we are seeing today. These are leaders in their fields and that also helps to warrant the relatively higher valuations, but we want to see continued growth to back this up before we consider an investment. For now, IETC is a hold as its companies are some of the best in class.

data.title: 2025 Midyear Investment Outlook: The Global Reset
data.description:
The global economic and political landscape is shifting rapidly, marked by a broad reordering of trade relations and political alliances around the globe. In response, uncertainty measures across global markets soared in the first half of 2025.

We make no apologies for acknowledging that there are plenty of things we do not know today. We remain wary about precise estimates of where tariff rates will settle, the exact timing of interest rate changes and detailed inflation and growth forecasts. These estimates, among others, are heavily dependent on a more consistent sense of US policy direction.

That said, we have greater confidence in the direction of travel for some key trends, macro factors, and, ultimately, markets. We expect tariffs to be higher than in previous decades and US immigration to be lower. We expect fiscal spending on defense and infrastructure to be greater in Europe. The result is likely to be slower growth and higher inflation in the US in 2025 than was expected at the start of the year. Similarly, growth may slow outside the US but to a lesser degree. A better-than-feared resolution of tariff disputes and the positive impact of anticipated deregulation may continue to allow US markets to rally.

Looking beyond the tariff headlines

While US politics dominated the news flow in the first half of 2025, it is important to note that there have been developments elsewhere in the world that would have been the “story of the year” in more normal times.

In March, German Chancellor Friedrich Merz pledged to do “whatever it takes” to ensure the defense of Europe, releasing Germany’s debt brake and freeing the country to engage in greater infrastructure and defense spending. This bold move should provide a positive tailwind for European growth over the next decade.

China, too, is engaging in greater fiscal spending, and there are signs of improvement in the property and consumer sectors.

These green shoots are a further sign that while US tariffs will likely remain a drag on global growth, other factors are becoming more supportive of better growth outside of the US.

Watching the central banks

The US Federal Reserve (Fed) is in a tough bind. While most of the usual hard data point to keeping rates on hold, soft data point to an impending slowdown that could justify rate cuts. It is likely that US rates will stay on hold for a while longer, but then be cut aggressively in the event of a significant slowdown in activity.

Other central banks have an easier task, since US tariffs and a weaker dollar will likely add to disinflationary pressure in regions outside of the US and spur quicker and more cuts than were priced at the start of the year. Cuts from the European Central Bank are already helping European consumers who now have greater confidence to save less and spend more.

Of course, the Bank of Japan is the one major central bank that appears to be still on a tightening path. Further interest rate hikes may be delayed until the end of 2025 or early 2026. But we think more will come, just as other central banks ease. We suspect this will continue to support the Japanese yen.

Our base case: Non-US assets increasingly attractive

So, while policy and economic uncertainty are high and there is much we cannot say for certain, we are confident in our base case that non-US assets are increasingly attractive and poised for continued outperformance. We view this as an opportunity for investors to diversify their portfolios across regions and asset classes, as well as to reduce concentrations. This may help in weathering volatility while also allowing investors to benefit from potential upside surprises.

Scenarios: How does the trade war evolve from here?

Base case: Uncertainty continues

  • US domestic policy volatility and uncertainty are likely to persist for the remainder of 2025.
  • US tariffs remain at multi-decade highs but well below levels initially announced on “Liberation Day,” and US-China trading relations gradually improve.
  • These combined effects likely cause a mild slowdown in the US economy, although the extension of tax cuts and deregulation could provide a tailwind.
  • Disinflationary pressures in Europe and China should allow governments and central banks to stimulate their domestic economies.

Favored assets

  • Equities: European equities, UK equities, Asian equities
  • Fixed income: Global ex-US bonds (corporate and sovereign), Local currency emerging market bonds
  • Alternatives: Private credit (including real estate), hedged strategies, industrial metals
  • Currencies: Euro, British pound

Downside scenario: Geopolitical breakdown

  • US trade policy triggers reciprocal tariffs from other nations, and limited deals are negotiated.
  • Geopolitical tensions escalate further, with imports to the US falling significantly. This may entail a further breakdown of the international order and/or a significant rupture of relations between the US and China.
  • The US enters a recession, and global growth experiences a significant slowdown, while tariffs elsewhere push up prices outside of the US.

Favored assets

  • Equities: Non-US low volatility and defensives, especially utilities and telecoms
  • Fixed income: Sovereign debt, especially non-US
  • Alternatives: Potentially attractive entry point for distressed debt and special situations. Hedged strategies, gold and precious metals
  • Currencies: Japanese yen, Swiss franc

Upside scenario: Policy and trade war reprieve

  • The US administration engages in a policy pivot, tempering tariff and immigration policy while focusing on more pro-growth policies. (potentially due to Congress reining in executive trade authority).
  • A partial normalization of trade policy results in an incomplete return to the pre-2025 state.
  • Growth outlook improves materially outside of the US and offsets a mild US slowdown.
  • US-China relations improve.

Favored assets

  • Equities: Value, small and mid cap
  • Fixed income: US investment grade, US high yield
  • Alternatives: Private equity and real estate equity, collateralized loan obligation equity, industrial commodities
  • Currencies: US dollar, commodity currencies (Australian dollar, Canadian dollar)

Important information

NA4544828

All investing involves risk, including the risk of loss.

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

Diversification does not guarantee a profit or eliminate the risk of loss.

In general, stock values fluctuate, sometimes widely, in response to activities specific to the company as well as general market, economic and political conditions.

The risks of investing in securities of foreign issuers, including emerging market issuers, can include fluctuations in foreign currencies, political and economic instability, and foreign taxation issues.

Investments in companies located or operating in Greater China are subject to the following risks: nationalization, expropriation, or confiscation of property, difficulty in obtaining and/or enforcing judgments, alteration or discontinuation of economic reforms, military conflicts, and China’s dependency on the economies of other Asian countries, many of which are developing countries.

Stocks of small- and mid-sized companies tend to be more vulnerable to adverse developments, may be more volatile, and may be illiquid or restricted as to resale.

Alternative products typically hold more non-traditional investments and employ more complex trading strategies, including hedging and leveraging through derivatives, short selling and opportunistic strategies that change with market conditions. Investors considering alternatives should be aware of their unique characteristics and additional risks from the strategies they use. Like all investments, performance will fluctuate. You can lose money.

Commodities may subject an investor to greater volatility than traditional securities such as stocks and bonds and can fluctuate significantly based on weather, political, tax, and other regulatory and market developments.

Fixed income investments are subject to credit risk of the issuer and the effects of changing interest rates. Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa. An issuer may be unable to meet interest and/or principal payments, thereby causing its instruments to decrease in value and lowering the issuer’s credit rating.

High yield bonds, or junk bonds, involve a greater risk of default or price changes due to changes in the issuer’s credit quality. The values of junk bonds fluctuate more than those of high quality bonds and can decline significantly over short time periods.

Fluctuations in the price of gold and precious metals may affect the profitability of companies in the gold and precious metals sector. Changes in the political or economic conditions of countries where companies in the gold and precious metals sector are located may have a direct effect on the price of gold and precious metals.

Investments in real estate-related instruments may be affected by economic, legal, or environmental factors that affect property values, rents or occupancies of real estate. Real estate companies, including REITs or similar structures, tend to be small and mid-cap companies and their shares may be more volatile and less liquid.

Tightening monetary policy includes actions by a central bank to curb inflation.

Green shoots is a term used to describe signs of economic recovery during an economic downturn.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of May. 30, 2025. These comments should not be construed as recommendations, but as an illustration of broader themes. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties and assumptions; there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.

2025 midyear investment outlook: The global reset by Invesco US

data.title: Ford Motor: Solid EV Momentum
data.description:
The stock price of Ford Motor Company (NYSE:F) has been hovering around $10 pretty much since August 2024 though the auto company is growing its EV sales and inflation has been moderating quite substantially, leading to profit upside.

While high inflation was a considerable challenge for U.S. auto manufacturers to overcome in 2023 and 2024, primarily because of higher materials and labor costs, retreating inflation creates a catalyst for profit growth, as does solid momentum in terms of EV sales.

In my last stock classification for Ford Motor, I pointed to the auto company’s 6% yield as one reason why investors would want to gobble up a few shares for their passive income portfolios. The scaling of electric-vehicle deliveries, in a low-inflation environment, is now one possible catalyst for accelerating profit growth for Ford Motor and estimates for 2026 are looking quite good.

Improving Model E EBIT Margin Trend, Narrowing Of EV Unit Losses

Electric vehicles sales have not been growing as fast as anticipated, with consumers slowing their rate of purchases, particularly in 2024, amid challenges to consumer spending.

High inflation and trade uncertainty have weighed on consumers’ willingness to make large purchases, such as new pickup trucks or SUVs. That being said, Ford Motor is still seeing quite robust growth in its electric-vehicle division, which is going to remain key for the auto company to produce some healthy profit growth moving forward.

Inflation has seen a rapid downturn so far in the last couple of months, with data showing a third consecutive monthly decline in the pace of inflation this year. Inflation in April stood at an annualized 2.3% and inflation cooled particularly quickly in both March and April. For U.S. auto companies like Ford Motor, falling inflation and rising EV sales, could mean that the profit outlook is improving.

In 2025, all eyes should be on Ford Motor’s electric-vehicle division, which is still racking up a good amount of losses, but the auto company is some considerable momentum here.

In the first quarter, Ford Motor’s electric-vehicle unit produced an EBIT loss of $0.8 billion, which may not be great news for investors as a loss is still a loss, and quite a substantial one at that. That said, however, losses decreased by a whopping $0.6 billion QoQ and I think that if Ford Motor maintains its present delivery momentum, the auto company has a good shot at outperforming its $7.0-8.5 billion EBIT target for 2025.

EBIT margins in Ford Motor’s electric-vehicle segment are improving at a quick pace which comes on the back of higher deliveries and the starting effects of economies of scale.

Still, Ford Model e weighs on Ford’s total EBIT margins, but as the company scales its delivery volumes and launches higher margin products, I think the company could potentially surprise in terms of narrowing its segment losses this year.

Though it is true that electric-vehicle sales momentum has slowed a bit in 2024, it is clear from industry data, as well as Ford Motor’s reports, that the auto company is growing its deliveries at a remarkable pace.

In 1Q25, Ford Motor’s EV sales increased 12% YoY, but the auto company compares particularly favorably against Tesla Inc. (TSLA) which exhibited a decrease in their sales volumes of 9% YoY during that same quarter.

Tesla has been harshly punished by consumers in the U.S. and Europe this year, which is partially attributable to his acting as a high-profile advisor to President Trump. Since Elon Musk has since publicly dialed back his exposure to partisan politics, I think that Tesla’s stock is still very much compelling as a long-term EV investment, despite its sky-high valuation multiple.

Though it is true that electric-vehicle sales momentum has slowed a bit in 2024, it is clear from industry data, as well as Ford Motor’s reports, that the auto company is growing its deliveries at a remarkable pace. In 1Q25, Ford Motor’s EV sales increased 12% YoY, but the auto company compares particularly favorably against Tesla Inc. (TSLA) which exhibited a decrease in their sales volumes of 9% YoY during that same quarter.

Tesla has been harshly punished by consumers in the U.S. and Europe this year, which is partially attributable to his acting as a high-profile advisor to President Trump. Since Elon Musk has since publicly dialed back his exposure to partisan politics, I think that Tesla’s stock is still very much compelling as a long-term EV investment, despite its sky-high valuation multiple.

Ford Motor’s Juicy 6% Yield Might Be Undervalued

If you are looking for a robust 6% dividend yield from an S&P 500 company, Ford Motor should be very high on your shopping or watch-list. Ford Motor is paying investors $0.15 per share per quarter, but has also paid special dividends in the past. In the last three years, Ford Motor paid special dividends varying in size (the most recent one of $0.15 per share) in March 2025.

Ford Motor’s valuation reflects a high safety margin as its stocks are changing hands for only 7.7x 2026e profits while the auto company is anticipated to grow its profits by 26%. Ford Motor’s arch-rival, General Motors Company (GM), is selling for 5.1x 2026 estimated profits, but Ford Motor is poised to outshine General Motors in terms of profit growth by a whopping 24 percentage points.

Pure EV-play Tesla is selling for 104.6x next year’s estimated profits and is expected to see 51% profit growth YoY thanks partially to the re-launch of a newer version of Tesla’s best-selling Model Y.

That said, many investors are not particularly eager to invest in Tesla right now, mainly because we don’t yet whether or not the consumer strike in 1Q25 will have a lasting effect. Tesla has also disappointed in recent years in terms of its delayed robotaxi.

From a yield angle, however, I think that Ford Motor is an exceptionally interesting stock; it presently pays passive income investors a solid 5.8% dividend yield and though the dividend is not growing, the yield itself is about 4.5 times higher than the S&P 500 yield.

In addition, EV momentum is still benefiting Ford Motor and with a potential acceleration of growth in the electric-vehicle segment, catalyzed by receding inflation and a boost to consumer spending, the auto company could even be putting stronger-than-anticipated results on the table in 2025.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Be, Against My Expectations, More Bearish

Ford Motor’s transition to electric vehicles is not going as smooth or as fast as the market predicted two or three years ago. Auto companies including Ford Motor and General Motors have scaled back their investment activities with regards to EV transitions because of slowing adoption of electric vehicles in the market, but the market is still growing, benefiting companies that made past investments in manufacturing capacity.

With Ford Motor’s EV sales still growing and the stock paying a handsome dividend, I think that the risk/reward relationship will ultimately benefit long-term investors.

My Takeaway

Ford Motor might offer passive income investors a better investment package than a first glance would indicate. The auto company is scaling up its electric-vehicle deliveries and its EV division losses are narrowing, which could set the auto company up for potential earnings surprises as well as stronger-than-anticipated EBIT.

Though the Model e segment is still very much anticipated to be a loss-leader for Ford Motor in 2025, I think that the context of moderating inflation and surging wholesale [EV] volumes is potentially creating a favorable setup for the auto company to accelerate its profit growth.

Importantly, the stock of Ford Motor is not particularly expensive, so its valuation reflects a high margin of safety already. Buy.

data.title: Municipals: Relative Value In U.S. Fixed Income
data.description:
By Kevin Flanagan

While uncertainty will continue to hover over the money and bond markets in the months ahead, the good news is that investors have been given a scenario where yield levels have returned to "normal" levels. This development has provided a "new" landscape to consider when putting money to work in the U.S. fixed arena. In my opinion, one sector that currently offers relative value is the municipal (muni) bond market.

Relative Value Ratio

Typically, when analyzing the muni market, one key gauge that is focused on is known as the "relative value ratio." This ratio is simply the percentage of the yield offered on a muni security as compared to a U.S. Treasury (UST) security, with the UST 10-Year being the traditional comparison. The general rule of thumb is that the higher this percentage is, the cheaper and better relative value munis will be. Remember, this is before taxes are taken into consideration…more on that aspect down below.

Muni Relative Value Ratios

The graph above outlines relative ratios over the last nearly 2 ½ years for the three key sectors of the muni market: AAA Callable, AA Revenue and A Revenue. As you can see, the ratios have been on the rise here in 2025, i.e., munis have underperformed the UST 10-Year. In terms of the ratios themselves, each one is about 10 percentage points above its average respective readings during this period.

In fact, as of this writing, municipals are the only major U.S. fixed income sector that has produced a negative return (-1.1%) thus far this year. Three things come to mind for the reason behind this underperformance: increased supply, seasonal selling during tax season and concerns about whether the "big, beautiful bill" would change the federal tax-exempt status of munis. The first two reasons tend to sort themselves out each year, but more importantly, the House-passed version kept this tax-exempt status intact, and it is highly likely the final version of the "bb" bill will as well.

Tax-Equivalent Yield (TEY)

That brings us to the tax-equivalent yield aspect of munis. The TEY enables investors to compare the yield on taxable and tax-exempt securities and represents the yield an investment in taxable bonds would have to earn in order to match, after federal income taxes, the yield available on an investment in tax-exempt municipal bonds.

WisdomTree recently launched two municipal Funds, the WisdomTree Core Laddered Municipal Fund (WTMU) and the WisdomTree High Income Laddered Municipal Fund (WTMY), in conjunction with industry experts Insight Investment. As their titles specify, these two Funds utilize the time-tested laddered approach to fixed income investing and are active solutions.

The TEY for a fund is based on the fund's SEC 30-day yield and uses the highest marginal federal income tax rate. State income and alternative minimum taxes are excluded from the calculation.

Tax-Equivalent Municipal SEC Yields vs. U.S. Fixed Income

The chart above clearly illustrates how the TEYs for both WTMU and WTMY are visibly above the U.S. investment-grade fixed income universe. An investor would need to move strictly into all high-yield corporates in order to find a higher-yielding instrument.

Conclusion

In my opinion, the municipal market appears to be telling investors that, besides the traditional tax-advantaged solution, munis could also be considered for broader fixed income portfolios.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. Municipal securities carry various risks, including credit, interest rate, prepayment and valuation risks. Issuers may face financial difficulties that impact their ability to meet payment obligations. The value of these securities can fluctuate due to changes in revenue sources, local economic and political conditions and industry-specific downturns (e.g., education, health care, transportation, utilities). Additionally, tax-exempt income from municipal securities could become taxable due to regulatory changes or issuer noncompliance, potentially reducing their value. Fixed income investments are subject to interest rate risk; their value will normally decline as interest rates rise. Higher-yield securities or “junk” bonds have lower credit ratings and involve a greater risk to principal. Fixed income investments are also subject to credit risk, the risk that the issuer of a bond will fail to pay interest and principal in a timely manner or that negative perceptions of the issuer’s ability to make such payments will cause the price of that bond to decline. While the Fund attempts to limit credit and counterparty exposure, the value of an investment in the Fund may change quickly and without warning in response to issuer or counterparty defaults and changes in the credit ratings of the Fund’s portfolio investments. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Kevin Flanagan, Head of Fixed Income Strategy

As part of WisdomTree’s Investment Strategy group, Kevin serves as Head of Fixed Income Strategy. In this role, he contributes to the asset allocation team, writes fixed income-related content and travels with the sales team, conducting client-facing meetings and providing expertise on WisdomTree’s existing and future bond ETFs. In addition, Kevin works closely with the fixed income team. Prior to joining WisdomTree, Kevin spent 30 years at Morgan Stanley, where he was Managing Director and Chief Fixed Income Strategist for Wealth Management. He was responsible for tactical and strategic recommendations and created asset allocation models for fixed income securities. He was a contributor to the Morgan Stanley Wealth Management Global Investment Committee, primary author of Morgan Stanley Wealth Management’s monthly and weekly fixed income publications, and collaborated with the firm’s Research and Consulting Group Divisions to build ETF and fund manager asset allocation models. Kevin has an MBA from Pace University’s Lubin Graduate School of Business, and a B.S in Finance from Fairfield University.

Original Post

data.title: Academy Sports and Outdoors: Staying Neutral Until There Is More Near-Term Outlook Clarity
data.description:
Investment overview

I give a hold rating for Academy Sports and Outdoors, Inc. (NASDAQ:ASO), as I am worried about the near-term outlook. Execution on store expansion, premium brand partnerships, and higher-income customer traction is encouraging. However, weakness among core lower-income consumers, inventory risks, and macro uncertainties create near-term uncertainties. I prefer to wait for more stable consumer trends or tariff clarity before getting more bullish.

Company Description

ASO is a sporting goods and outdoor recreation retailer in the US. As of 1Q25, it has 303 stores across 21 states. The business model is to offer value-driven products across outdoor, sports & recreation, apparel, and footwear categories. The distinctive aspect is that ASO focuses its product assortments with a mix of top national brands and exclusive private-label brands. This allows ASO to serve a broad range of customers—from casual families to dedicated athletes.

1Q25 Earnings Review

ASO 1Q25 results were mixed. Net sales fell 0.9% y/y to $1.35 billion, and comparable sales fell 3.7%. The poor sales performance was attributed to the weather, and management pointed out that trends have improved each month in the quarter, ending with positive comparable sales in April. Gross margin did well, though, expanding by 60 bps y/y to 34% on stronger merchandise margins and lower shrink. Unfortunately, that expansion was offset by higher operating costs. SG&A grew 10.2% y/y, as ASO spent on new-store openings, tech upgrades, and marketing around key brand launches. The net result was adj. EPS of $0.76.

Positive takeaway

I will start with the positive takeaway I have for ASO. Firstly, the store expansion plan is progressing very well. Shops opened in 2022 and 2023 are already posting low-single-digit comparable sales growth—which beats ASO consolidated store base performance. Management also noted that the 2024 class is off to a great start, which suggests this low-single-digits trend has sustained. This traction gives me confidence new units can drive top-line growth.

Secondly, traffic from households earning >$100k grew double digits in 1Q25, and it has been a trend over the last several quarters. This shows that ASO is benefiting directly from the trade-down motion, wherein higher-income customers are facing the heat from the poor macro backdrop and are looking for more value alternatives. This also has long-term growth implications. If ASO can take this opportunity to show this richer demographic that its products are not of inferior quality (just deemed as value choices), it could hold on to this market share over time.

Thirdly, the latest Nike launch (Jordan Brand in 145 stores in late April) could continue to be a near-term growth driver. These new products have been attracting a lot of heat, and we know this because despite being just in stores for 2 weeks of the quarter, the sales performance already exceeded management’s expectations, and the trend has sustained into May. This launch also has a broader implication on ASO business, as the launch of the Jordan brand raises ASO's profile with premium vendors and potentially opens the door to other popular brands.

Core Consumer and Macro Headwinds Remain

While ASO is gaining traction with its store expansion strategy and higher-income shoppers, there are issues that, I believe, may weigh heavily on the near-term stock sentiment. Firstly, store traffic from lower-income households (particularly those earning <$50,000) is declining. Remember that at ASO’s core, this is a value retailer, and the lower-income customer cohort is its key customer base. Also, management also pointed out cautious spending among Hispanic consumers, which is a key demographic for the company. These are not the signs that I like to see for a value retailer like ASO. If anything, this tells me that the negative impact of the ongoing trade-down motion (lower-income customers stop buying completely) has started to outweigh the positive impacts (higher-income customers trading down). This is exceptionally worrying when we consider that ASO is expanding its store base today. While this could help gain share over the long term, the near-term impact on earnings could be huge as ASO core customers continue to spend less (more stores = higher fixed costs).

Also, despite the Jordan launch and comparable sales trend picking up in April, May sales returned to a low-single-digit decline. This tells me that the demand remains very volatile and makes the near-term growth difficult to forecast. If we combine these sales concerns with the elevated inventory that ASO is carrying, it becomes more concerning. Inventory grew 15% y/y in 1Q25, which is significantly above the 0.9% sales decline. Some of these inventories ($85 million worth) are pulled forward ahead of potential tariff hikes, and while this may offer cost advantages if tariffs are imposed, it also increases the risk of future markdowns in a soft demand environment. If sales trends fail to improve, there is a good chance that ASO may be forced to discount these inventories, which could pressure margins and hurt profitability.

Valuation

For valuation, I don’t think the risk/reward is attractive enough to justify investors taking the risks I mentioned above. ASO is valued at ~7.3x NTM PE, and this is near the historical trading average over the past 5 years. It could really go both ways, in my opinion. If tariff risks go away, the US macro situation gets better with lower inflation and interest rates, and the tax bill passes, consumer demand could really pick up and drive demand for ASO. In this scenario, I wouldn’t be surprised to see the NTM PE go up to 9x (+1 standard deviation). However, the flip side is true as well. Based on the current situation, inflation is at risk of surging again due to tariffs, and rates are still high. Tariff negotiations are also uncertain. If things turn south, ASO could easily derate back to where it traded earlier this year at 6x NTM PE.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating for ASO. The company is executing well on expanding its store base, strengthening brand partnerships, and attracting more affluent shoppers. But these positives are offset by weakening demand from its core customer base, uncertain sales trends, and growing inventory in a softening environment. I think investors are better off staying on the sidelines for now until visibility improves.

data.title: U.S. Investor Risk Aversion Extends To 5th Straight Month In June
data.description:
Overall US investor sentiment leaned toward risk aversion for the fifth month in a row, even as caution in equity markets diminished for a third straight month.

The results of the monthly S&P Global Investment Manager Index US equity investor sentiment survey, released June 10, showed the Risk Appetite Index rose to negative 13% in June from negative 19% in May and negative 31% in April. Though sentiment has improved each month since a recent low point in March, the index in June continued its stretch of negative readings, indicating risk aversion that began in February. The index measures net risk appetite among surveyed investors, with those reporting a high tolerance or aversion counting with double weight.

Equity markets were volatile in March and April as the US readied new tariffs on nearly all major trading partners. Stocks have since rebounded from an April sell-off after most tariffs were delayed until July while global governments pursued trade negotiations with the US. Still, market caution persisted as negotiations played out and market observers pivoted their attention to a budget bill now being debated by the US Congress.

"While the survey has seen some calming of worries over tariffs, the political environment remains the biggest drag on equity performance," Chris Williamson, executive director at S&P Global Market Intelligence, said in a report released alongside the survey results. "Fiscal worries surrounding the deficit and cuts in spending in areas such as Medicare are also driving risk aversion, with little scope for any boost to equities meanwhile seen from US Federal Reserve rate cuts."

Near-term equity losses still expected

Even with the easing in risk aversion, investors expect net market losses in the coming month.

The survey's Equity Returns Index slipped to negative 32% in June from negative 29% in May. The score reflects the percentage of surveyed respondents expecting an improvement in equity returns over the 30 days following the end of the survey, minus the percentage of those expecting net equity losses. June's survey score indicated a slightly more pessimistic outlook than in May.

For the full year, investors anticipate a modest year-over-year gain for the S&P 500, with the median expectation projecting the index will close out the year at 6,000. This would be a 2% improvement from 2024, compared with the 23% surge from 2023 to 2024.

Surveyed respondents indicated that only shareholder returns were likely to contribute to positive market performance in June, when compared with seven other market-influencing factors. By contrast, most surveyed investors believed that the political environment, high stock valuations and government fiscal policy would place the most pressure on equity returns.

The index readings for political environment and stock valuations have consistently been the lowest this year among all market factors.

The survey index score of negative 33% in June for the US macroeconomic environment improved 29 percentage points from May. Though the outlook remained pessimistic, this was the biggest improvement in sentiment among all eight market factors.

IT emerges as most preferred sector

Investors signaled bullishness in six of 11 industries in June, with the greatest preference for information technology, according to the survey's sector index.

Sentiment toward the industrial and communication sectors flipped from negative in May to positive in June, with the communication sector showing as the second-most preferred sector overall this month.

The energy sector was reported to have the largest improvement in index score in June, with a 34-percentage-point increase month over month, though investor net outlook for the sector remained negative.

Sentiment for the healthcare sector was the only one to deteriorate to negative in June from positive in May. This is the first time in the survey's five-year history that investors reported bearishness in the sector. The survey report attributed the pessimistic outlook to concerns over potential Medicare cuts in the US.

The consumer discretionary sector was the most out of favor with investors in June, followed by real estate. The real estate sector's index score in June dropped the most month over month, with a 19-percentage-point decrease to negative 35%.
